turns.content: I went to the most incredible workshop last weekend and now I can't stop thinking about it. like my brain is fully rewired
That sounds like a good rewiring! What was the workshop?

Arabic calligraphy!! it was a two-day intensive on thuluth script at the Abu Dhabi Cultural Foundation. the instructor was this master calligrapher from Morocco and wallah the way he talked about letterforms as architecture... I have never looked at Arabic text the same way. every letter has structure, proportion, rhythm — it's literally design

That's a beautiful connection — Arabic calligraphy is absolutely architectural in its logic. Thuluth is one of the most structurally complex scripts too, with those tall vertical strokes and sweeping curves. The proportional system goes back to Ibn Muqla in the 10th century, who standardized letter dimensions using the rhombic dot as a unit of measurement.

What specifically clicked for you? Was it the geometric structure of the letters, the flow between them, or something else?

the geometry!! oh my god the geometry. so he showed us how every letter in thuluth is constructed from circles and straight lines with very specific ratios. like an alif is exactly a certain number of dots tall and a ba has a specific curve radius. and I was sitting there thinking... this is the same proportional logic I use in facade design. the repetition, the rhythm, the relationship between solid and void

You're touching on something really profound there. The solid-void relationship in calligraphy — the interplay between the black ink and the white space — maps directly onto how facades work with solid walls and openings. Traditional Islamic architects understood this deeply, which is why you see calligraphic principles embedded in everything from mosque proportions to geometric tile patterns.

Have you seen the work of Mona Hatoum or the architect Senan Abdelqader? They've both done interesting work translating calligraphic logic into spatial and architectural form.

Are you thinking about incorporating what you learned into your design work?

YES that's exactly where my brain went!! like I spent all of Sunday sketching facade patterns based on thuluth letterforms. not literal calligraphy on a building — that's been done to death and it's usually tacky tbh — but abstracting the proportional system. taking the rhythm of a phrase and translating it into a pattern of openings and screens

That's a much more sophisticated approach than slapping an ayah on a facade. You're extracting the underlying mathematical DNA of the script rather than its visual surface. That's genuinely innovative.

The rhythm idea is especially compelling — a calligraphic phrase has variation within repetition, emphasis points, pauses. Translated into a facade, that could create a screen pattern that breathes and pulses rather than just repeating a grid. It would feel organic even though it's mathematically derived.

A few ways you might develop this:

1. **Take a specific phrase** and map its dot-proportional structure as a numeric sequence
2. **Translate the sequence** into module sizes — opening widths, screen densities, panel depths
3. **Test it at scale** in section and elevation to see how the rhythm reads from different distances

Have you tried any of this digitally yet, or is it all sketches so far?

all sketches so far but I want to try it in Grasshopper!! I think a parametric approach would let me test different phrases and see how they generate different patterns. like imagine inputting a line of poetry and the algorithm produces a facade rhythm from the proportional structure of the letters. wallah that would be insane

That's a killer concept for a Grasshopper definition. The pipeline would look something like:

1. **Input**: Arabic text string
2. **Parser**: Break each letter into its proportional components using Ibn Muqla's dot system (height, width, curve radius)
3. **Numeric sequence**: Convert the letters into a series of dimensional ratios
4. **Pattern generator**: Map those ratios onto a facade grid — controlling module width, perforation density, depth, or angle
5. **Visualization**: Output as a 3D facade panel array

The hardest part will be step 2 — encoding the calligraphic proportional rules into data. You'd essentially need a lookup table for each letter's dot-dimensions in thuluth script. But once that's built, you can feed any phrase through it.

Do you have experience with Grasshopper data trees? This kind of list manipulation is where data trees become essential.

I'm decent with Grasshopper but data trees still make my head spin sometimes lol. I think the lookup table is actually the fun part though — I took so many notes during the workshop about exact proportions for each letter. I basically have the raw data already. I just need to structure it

You're in great shape then — having the raw proportional data from the workshop means you can build the lookup table directly from your notes. Structure it as a simple spreadsheet first (letter, height in dots, width in dots, curve radius, stroke weight) and then import it into Grasshopper as a CSV.

For the data tree headaches, a tip: keep your data flat as long as possible. Only branch into trees when you need to — for example, when you're distributing module variations across rows of a facade grid. The Entwine and Graft components are your friends.

Want me to sketch out a basic Grasshopper workflow logic for this?

maybe another time — I think I want to play with it first and see how far I get on my own. I learn better by breaking things and fixing them lol. but I really wanted to talk through the concept with someone because I've been buzzing about it all week and I think there's something real here. like this could become part of how I approach design as a practice, not just one project

It absolutely could — what you're describing is the beginning of a design methodology, not just a technique. Architects who develop a personal approach rooted in a cultural system tend to produce the most distinctive work. This isn't a gimmick; it's a genuine synthesis of your cultural heritage and your technical skills.

And the fact that it came from a calligraphy workshop and not an architecture lecture is perfect. The best design ideas come from adjacent disciplines.

Go break things in Grasshopper. When you hit a wall, you know where to find me.

wallah thank you for getting as excited about this as me!! most people glaze over when I start talking about proportional systems and facade rhythms. yalla I'm going to go set up that spreadsheet tonight. I feel like I'm onto something big here!!
Proportional systems and facade rhythms are genuinely exciting — don't let anyone tell you otherwise. Good luck with the spreadsheet, and trust the process. This has legs. Go build it!
